q: Alpha decay ($\alpha$ decay) is a process in which an atomic nucleus emits an alpha particle (helium nucleus) and transforms into a different atomic nucleus, with mass number reduces by four and atomic number reduces by two.
In alpha decay, the mass number of the parent nucleus is reduced by two.
Atomic number and mass number are conserved in the decay process.
$\beta^-$ decay (beta-minus decay) is a process in which a neutron in an atomic nucleus changes to a proton with the emission of an electron ($\beta^-$) and an antineutrino ($\bar{\nu}_e$).
In $ eta^-$ decay, the parent nucleus transforms into the daughter nucleus, with mass number remains unchanged but atomic number increases by one.
$\beta^+$ decay (beta-plus decay) is a process in which a proton in an atomic nucleus changes to a neutron with the emission of a positron ($\beta^+$) and a neutrino ($\nu_e$).
In $ eta^+$ decay, the mass number of the parent nucleus increases by one.
Positron, the antiparticle of electron, has the same mass and same magnitude of electric charge as an electron.
Positron is negatively charged.
The existence of positron was theoretically predicted by Dirac in 1931 and experimentally detected by Anderson in 1932.
Neutrino, a fundamental particle as an electron, has no charge and negligibly small rest mass ($ < 2 \times 10^{-37}\text{ kg}$).
Each neutrino is associated with an antineutrino.
For a nucleus to be stable, there must be an appropriate ratio of protons and neutrons.
If a nucleus has excess neutrons, $\beta^+$ decay takes place.
A nucleus with excess protons undergoes $\beta^+$ decay.
In gamma decay ($\gamma$ decay), the daughter nucleus resulting from alpha or beta decay is left in an excited state and it can then decay to a lower energy state by emitting a gamma ray photon.
In gamma decay, the atomic number and mass number of the parent and daughter nuclei remain unchanged.
Gamma decay takes place when a nucleus at an excited state deexcites to a lower excited state or to the ground state.
Photons are massless and move at the speed of light in vacuum.
The energy of a photon is directly proportional to the wavelength of the electromagnetic radiation associated with it.
Alpha rays consist of helium nuclei, positively charged particles.
An $\alpha$ particle consists of two protons and two neutrons.
The charge of an $\alpha$ particle is $+2 e$.
Alpha rays are the least ionizing of the three rays.
Alpha rays are the least penetrating of the three rays.
Alpha rays can be stopped by a thick sheet of paper.
Beta rays consist of either electrons (charge $= -e$) or positrons (charge $= +e$) moving with varying speeds.
Beta rays are more ionizing than alpha rays.
Beta rays can be stopped by a few millimetres of aluminium.
Gamma rays are electromagnetic waves like light and X-rays, but have much shorter wavelengths.
Gamma rays have no charge.
Gamma rays can be deflected by electric and magnetic fields.
Intensity of gamma rays can be greatly reduced by several centimetres of lead but they are never completely stopped.
The rate of decay of a radioactive sample is called activity.
SI unit for activity is becquerel (Bq).
$1\text{ Bq} = 1\text{ event s}^{-1}$ (or 1 disintegration $\text{s}^{-1}$).
A unit of activity still being used today is curie (Ci).
The rate of decay (activity) is a characteristic of the radioactive element and it is affected by the temperature.
Activity of a radioactive sample decreases with time.
The half-life is defined as the time for half the atoms in a radioactive sample to decay.
Half-life of radioactive substances ranges from about femtosecond ($10^{-15}\text{ s}$) to billions of year.
Radioactive decay is a statistical process: it is impossible to predict when a particular atom will decay.
A radioisotope is an atom with an unstable nucleus which undergoes radioactive decay, resulting in the emission of alpha, beta and gamma rays.
A tracer is a small amount of radioactive isotope introduced into a system in order to follow the behaviour of some components of that system.
In medicine, radioactive tracers are effectively used in diagnostic procedures.
Treatment with radiation is known as radiotherapy.
Radioisotopes such as cobalt-60 and cesium-137 are widely used to treat cancer in radiotherapy.
In industrial gamma radiography, radioactive materials are used to inspect metal castings or welded joints.
Radioactive dating is a technique for estimating the age of an object by measuring the amounts of various radioisotopes present in it.
Carbon dating is a technique, developed by Willard Libby, for estimating the age of the remains of plants and animals.
The abundance of carbon-14 in the atmosphere is essentially constant.
There is one carbon-14 atom in every trillion ($10^{12}$) of carbon-12 atoms.
Lead is the stable final decay product of a series of decays that starts with uranium.
Gamma and neutron irradiation of seeds can be used to induce mutations to develop desired mutants.
